content.problemStatement:
It’s Chef's birthday. He and his twin have received NNN gifts in total. The iii-th gift has a price of AiA_iAi​. Each twin wants to keep the most expensive gifts for himself.

The twins take KKK turns alternately (each has KKK turns, for 2⋅K2\cdot K2⋅K turns in total). It is given that 2⋅K+1≤N2\cdot K+1 \leq N2⋅K+1≤N. In a turn, a person may choose one gift. The only exception is the last turn of the twin who moves second, where he gets to choose two gifts instead of one. Assuming they both choose gifts optimally and you can choose who takes the first turn, find the maximum total cost of the gifts that Chef keeps.

###Input


The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input.
The first line contains two space-separated integers NNN, KKK.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, the price of the gifts.
###Output
For each test case, output in a single line the answer to the problem.

###Constraints


1≤T≤1001 \leq T \leq 1001≤T≤100
3≤N≤1033 \leq N \leq 10^33≤N≤103
1≤K≤N−121 \leq K \leq \frac{N - 1}{2}1≤K≤2N−1​
1≤Ai≤1091 \leq A_i \leq 10^91≤Ai​≤109
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the prices of the gifts.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum total price Chef can obtain when he may choose to play first or second.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: Chef moves first and he chooses the gift with cost 333. His twin then chooses the gifts of costs 111 and 222.
Test Case 222: Chef allows his brother to choose first and his brother chooses a gift with cost 333. Then Chef chooses the remaining gift with cost 333. Since Chef moves second, he is allowed to choose one more gift, so he chooses gift with cost 111. The total cost of Chef's gifts is 3+1=43 + 1 = 43+1=4.
Test Case 333: Chef moves first and at the end he will have the gifts with costs 555 and 333. Hence, the total cost of gifts with Chef = 5+3=85 + 3 = 85+3=8.

solution.approach: Both twins always pick the most expensive remaining gift(s) because any deviation can only decrease their own total. Sort the prices in non‑increasing order. If Chef moves first, he receives the gifts at even positions 0,2,…,2K‑2 (K gifts). If Chef moves second, he receives the gifts at odd positions 1,3,…,2K‑3 (K‑1 gifts) and additionally the two gifts at positions 2K‑1 and 2K (the last turn where the second player picks two gifts). Compute both possible totals and take the maximum. Only the first 2K+1 elements of the sorted array matter.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting; O(N) additional work.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the sorted array.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); K = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort(reverse=True)
        # Chef first
        sum_first = sum(arr[2*i] for i in range(K))
        # Chef second
        sum_second = sum(arr[2*i+1] for i in range(K-1)) + arr[2*K-1] + arr[2*K]
        out.append(str(max(sum_first, sum_second)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
